name: Schumann resonance
note: The Earth–ionosphere cavity ringing at ≈7.83 Hz — the planet's natural EM heartbeat.

name: AC mains
note: Power-line hum — the 60 Hz that runs North America (50 Hz across much of the world).

name: Navy ELF
note: US Navy ELF — 76 Hz signalling that reaches submerged submarines, a few bits per minute.

name: VLF time/nav
note: Very low frequency (24 kHz) — global time/sync broadcasts that penetrate seawater to subs.

name: WWVB clock
note: WWVB 60 kHz time code — what 'atomic' wall clocks quietly sync to overnight.

name: Aviation NDB
note: Non-directional beacons (LF) — among the oldest radio navaids still in service.

name: AM radio
note: AM broadcast. Amplitude-modulated; long groundwave range at night.

name: 20 m ham
note: Premier HF DX band. CW, digital and SSB phone segments by licence class.

name: CB radio
note: Citizens Band — license-free voice, 40 channels, 4 W.

name: MRI RF
note: The RF pulse that flips proton spins inside a 1.5-tesla MRI magnet (≈64 MHz).

name: FM radio
note: FM broadcast. Wideband stereo, line-of-sight propagation.

name: NOAA wx sat
note: Polar weather-satellite image downlink (137 MHz APT) — receivable with a simple antenna.

name: 2 m ham
note: Local repeater / simplex band. Technician class and up.

name: FRS / GMRS
note: Family Radio — license-free walkie-talkies (GMRS needs a no-test permit).

name: TV (UHF)
note: Digital TV broadcast (ATSC). Also reused for cellular post-repack.

name: ADS-B
note: Aircraft position broadcast (1090 MHz). Trackable with a cheap SDR dongle.

name: GPS L1
note: GNSS positioning. Spread-spectrum, ~−130 dBm at the ground.

name: GOES sat
note: Geostationary weather-satellite imagery downlink (1.69 GHz).

name: Wi-Fi 2.4
note: Shared ISM band: Wi-Fi ch 1/6/11, Bluetooth, Zigbee, microwave ovens.

name: 5G n77
note: 5G mid-band — the capacity workhorse for modern cellular.

name: Wi-Fi 5
note: 5 GHz Wi-Fi — more channels, shorter range than 2.4 GHz.

name: Wx radar
note: Weather / marine radar (X-band, 9.4 GHz). Pulsed, high peak power.

name: 5G mmWave
note: 5G mmWave (n261, 28 GHz) — enormous capacity over a single city block.

name: WiGig 60
note: 60 GHz WiGig (802.11ad) — multi-gigabit links that oxygen absorption stops at the wall.

name: Cosmic microwave bg
note: The 2.7 K afterglow of the Big Bang — its blackbody spectrum peaks near 160 GHz.

name: Terahertz
note: Terahertz 'T-rays' (1 THz) — airport body scanners and the band science learned to use last.

name: IR heat
note: Thermal infrared (≈30 THz) — heat lamps, night vision, TV remotes nearby.

name: Fibre-optic telecom
note: Near-infrared (1550 nm) — the light that carries the internet through fibre under the oceans.

name: Visible light
note: The single octave the eye detects (390–700 nm) — a sliver of the whole spectrum.

name: UV-A
note: UV-A (≈365 nm) — blacklight glow, ink curing, and most of a suntan.

name: UV-C
note: Germicidal ultraviolet (≈1 PHz); also the cause of sunburn at longer UV.

name: EUV lithography
note: Extreme-UV (13.5 nm) — the tin-plasma light that prints today's microchips.

name: X-ray crystallography
note: Cu Kα X-rays (8 keV) — how we read the atomic structure of proteins and DNA.

name: Medical X-ray
note: Diagnostic radiography & CT (≈3 EHz). Ionising; tightly dose-controlled.

name: PET annihilation
note: Positron-annihilation γ-rays (511 keV) — the photons a PET scanner maps to find tumours.

name: Cobalt-60
note: Co-60 γ-rays (≈1.3 MeV) — sterilising medical kit and treating cancer.

name: Cosmic γ-rays
note: High-energy cosmic γ-rays (~400 MeV) — light from pulsars, blazars and supernova remnants.
